items.q: How do rivers transport their load?
items.correct: By traction (rolling large stones along the bed), saltation (bouncing smaller pebbles), suspension (carrying fine sediment in the flow) and solution (dissolved minerals).
items.misconception: Rivers transport everything by pushing it along the bottom.
items.random_wrong: Rivers can only transport material when they are in flood.
items.why: Students miss the four distinct processes. Particle size controls which one applies — and dissolved load is invisible but can be a huge proportion in limestone areas.

items.q: What is the ITCZ and why does it bring rain?
items.correct: The Intertropical Convergence Zone is where trade winds from both hemispheres converge near the equator, forcing air to rise, cool and produce heavy convectional rainfall.
items.misconception: The ITCZ is a fixed band of permanent storms at the equator.
items.random_wrong: The ITCZ is the boundary between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.
items.why: The ITCZ shifts seasonally north and south with the sun, driving monsoon rains. Students treat it as static — its movement explains why tropical regions have wet/dry seasons.

items.q: What is the difference between random, stratified and systematic sampling?
items.correct: Random: every member has equal chance of selection. Systematic: pick every nth item. Stratified: divide the population into groups and sample proportionally from each — ensures subgroup representation.
items.misconception: All three sampling methods produce the same results.
items.random_wrong: Stratified sampling means picking samples from different strata of rock.
items.why: Stratified sampling is for ensuring subgroups (e.g. age groups, land-use types) are proportionately represented. The choice depends on the population structure — wrong choice gives biased results.
The answer examiners want is: Composite. “Shield” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Fast hot gas and ash mix
items.why: Correct answer: Fast hot gas and ash mix. “Slow runny lava” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Pyroclastic flow can travel up to (km/h)?
items.why: Exact answer: 700. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
